name: Spicy and Sour Shrimp Soup
recipeIngredient: Lump tamarind or 2 tablespoons tamarind concentrate
Raw shrimp, shelled and deveined (8 oz)
Vietnamese fish sauce (1/4 cup plus 1 teaspoon nuoc mam)
Lemon grass stalks, white bulb crushed and cut into 2-inch sections
Ripe tomato (1 large), cored, seeded, and cut into wedges
Pineapple (1/4 fresh ripe), cored, cut into 1/4-inch slices, then crosswise into small chunks
Bamboo shoots (fresh or canned, drained and thinly sliced) (1/2 cup)
recipeInstructions:
This soup has become a staple on my table. It's relatively quick to make and absolutely delicious. I've made it with chicken as well as shrimp and have some red snapper in the freezer to try out the next time. Squid's a natural for this dish. It's from Southern Vietnam. You can make it as sour or spicy as you want by juggling the amounts of the ingredients that give those qualities. It makes a complete meal as is and is excellent with salad rolls or cha gio (fried "spring" rolls).
1. Soak the lump tamarind in boiling water for 15 minutes, or until the tamarind is soft.
2. Force the tamarind through a fine sieve into a small bowl. If using concentrate, dilute it with only 1/4 cup of warm water.
3. Cut each shrimp lengthwise in half. In a bowl, combine the shrimp, garlic, 1 teaspoon of fish sauce and pepper to taste. Let stand for 30 minutes.
4. Heat oil in a 3-quart saucepan. Add shallots and lemon grass and sauté briefly, without browning.
5. Add tomato and sugar and cook over moderate heat until slightly soft. Include pineapple and cook for about 2 minutes.
6. Add bamboo shoots and cook, stirring, for another 2 minutes. Add 5 cups of water and bring to a boil over high heat.
7. Stir in tamarind liquid, salt, and remaining fish sauce. Reduce heat to moderate and simmer the broth for 5 minutes.
8. Stir in shrimp, chiles, and bean sprouts; cook for 30 seconds more. Add scallion and mint. Remove from heat.
9. Discard lemon grass. Ladle soup into a heated tureen and serve immediately.
Note: Do not overcook the shrimp or they will toughen. Catfish, red snapper, or any other firm white-fleshed fish can replace the shrimp.
